title: Peppermint bark
subtitle: Layers of dark and white chocolate with peppermint extract
excerpt: Peppermint bark is a no-bake chocolate confection made by pouring a layer of dark chocolate, letting it set, then covering it with a white chocolate layer. Peppermint extract is stirred into both layers. It sets into a thin, snappable sheet that breaks into irregular shards. It takes about 20 minutes of active work.
sourceNotes: Peppermint bark is a North American confectionery tradition associated with the Christmas season, widely sold in US speciality food shops from the 1990s onward. The dark and white chocolate layered version became a signature Christmas gift food. This recipe is synthesised from the established American peppermint bark tradition.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale up proportionally. Use a larger tray and work in the same sequence.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for two weeks in an airtight container at room temperature in a cool place. Do not refrigerate as condensation will dull the chocolate surface.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no baking required; chocolate is melted over hot water and set at room temperature

glossaryTerms.definition: When adding the white chocolate layer to the set dark chocolate, the dark layer must be completely cold and firm. Pour the white chocolate at a working temperature of around 28 to 30 degrees C so it does not remelt the layer below. Spread it with a single light pass of a palette knife or the back of a spoon; going back and forth risks dragging and mixing the layers. If the white chocolate seizes when poured, the dark layer was still too cold or the layers are incompatible brands; start the white layer in a separate tin instead.

content.text: so the layers stay distinct. The dark layer must be fully cold before the white layer goes on. Keep any water well away from the melting chocolate: even a few drops of moisture will cause the chocolate to seize and turn grainy.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Melt the 200 g dark chocolate in a heatproof metal bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring occasionally. Remove from the heat and stir in half a teaspoon of the peppermint extract. Pour onto the lined tray and spread to an even layer about 5 mm thick. Refrigerate for 30 minutes until completely firm.
Add the white chocolate layer and set

Melt the 200 g white chocolate in the same way. Stir in the remaining half teaspoon peppermint extract. Leave to cool to about 30 degrees C, just above body temperature, then pour over the firm dark layer and spread quickly with a single light pass. Return to the fridge for 30 minutes until the white layer is set. Break into irregular shards. Store in an airtight container at room temperature.

items.problem: Layers mixed together rather than staying distinct
items.solution: The dark layer was not fully set before adding the white chocolate, or the white chocolate was too hot. Chill the dark layer until it is completely firm and ensure the white chocolate has cooled to around 30 degrees C before pouring.

Water contacted the chocolate during melting. Dry the bowl and spoon completely before use. If seizing has already started, add a teaspoon of vegetable oil and stir vigorously; this can sometimes smooth it out.
